context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus

Recommended due date: August 22 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
Create a DRAFT of 2 Climate Action Plan components for your case study city, building on your SWOT Analysis and findings about barriers to climate action. Each component should be 200-300 words. You'll share your research in the forum discussion and submit the final prospectus in Week 12.

When developing your policy recommendations, focus on Environmental Justice implications, vulnerabilities and environmental stressors in your city, and overlaps between different Climate Action Plan components.

Component Structure
For each of your 2 components, include the following sections:
COMPONENT [1 or 2]: [Policy Name]
• Context - Current situation and key challenge
• Opportunity - Specific opportunity for action
• Policy Recommendation - 200-300 words (your complete component)
• EJ Implications - Impact on vulnerable communities and equity measures
• Interactions - How this supports other climate actions and potential synergies

Assignment Completion Process
Review the examples of a Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus below for reference and before completing this worksheet. Use your completed components for forum discussion to refine your approach before the final submission.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on this climate action prospectus assignment. I picked a city, but I don’t really understand how to turn my SWOT and barriers stuff into 2 actual policy components.
exchanges.tutor: Good start picking a city! Before we dig in — what city did you choose, and can you share one or two key findings from your SWOT or barriers work? That'll help us figure out where to go from here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the starting point and feeling lost. Rather than explaining the structure to them, I should ask them to share what they already have (their city, SWOT, barriers) so I can meet them where they are and use guided discovery to help them connect their existing work to the policy components. Bite-sized, Socratic opening.

exchanges.student: It’s Miami, I think, but my notes are kind of all over the place. I had stuff about flooding, heat, housing inequality, and maybe transportation, so I can’t tell what counts as a “component” versus just a problem.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a solid set of findings for Miami. Here's a question to help you focus: what do you think is the difference between identifying a problem and proposing a policy component to address it? What does a component *do* that a problem statement doesn't?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has good raw material but is conflating problems with policy components. Rather than explaining the difference directly, I should ask a Socratic question that helps them see the distinction themselves — pushing them to think about what a 'component' actually does with a problem. I'll keep it short and pointed.

exchanges.student: I honestly don’t know, that’s where I’m stuck. It all just turns into “Miami has flooding so do something about flooding,” and then I’m not sure how detailed I’m supposed to be or what kind of thing even sounds like a policy.
exchanges.tutor: You're closer than you think. Try this: 'Miami has flooding' is the problem. Now ask yourself — who in Miami has the power to respond to that? And what specific action could they take? Even a rough, imperfect answer from you would help us move forward.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is genuinely stuck on the concept of what a policy recommendation looks like versus a problem statement. This is a foundational confusion that needs to be addressed before moving forward. Rather than explaining it directly, I can use a brief Socratic nudge that asks them to think about what 'doing something' actually means in concrete terms — who acts, how, with what tools. Keep it very bite-sized.

exchanges.student: Maybe the city government? Or transit people? Or housing people? That’s kind of why I’m confused, because it feels like everybody is involved and then I start thinking maybe I need to explain all of Miami first before I can write anything.
exchanges.tutor: You don't need to explain all of Miami — you just need to pick two problems and own them. Looking at your list — flooding, heat, housing inequality, transportation — which two feel most connected to each other? Sometimes the best components are ones that talk to each other.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is falling into an overthinking trap — wanting to explain everything before writing anything. I should gently push back on that instinct and redirect them toward making a choice and committing to it. The assignment only requires 2 components, so narrowing down is key. I'll use a pointed question to help them prioritize rather than expand.

exchanges.student: Maybe heat and housing inequality, but then flooding also seems huge, so I feel like I’m picking the wrong thing if I leave it out. I think I need a bigger-picture explanation of how these climate plan components are usually supposed to work, because I can’t really tell what belongs where.
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but a big-picture lecture isn't what you need right now — and the assignment actually gives you the structure already (Context, Opportunity, Policy Recommendation, EJ Implications, Interactions). There's no wrong choice between flooding and heat. Both are legitimate. Pick two and commit — you can always refine later. So: heat and housing, or flooding and housing? What's your gut?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give a broad explanation of how climate action plans work, which is a detour from the task. They're also showing decision paralysis about choosing topics. I should set a boundary on the detour, reassure them that there's no 'wrong' choice, and push them to commit to two topics so we can move forward. The assignment itself actually tells them what the component structure looks like, so I should redirect them there.

exchanges.student: I guess flooding and housing? But I still don’t really get what I’d put under each section without just repeating myself, and the “interactions” part especially is confusing.
exchanges.tutor: Good, flooding and housing it is. Let's focus on Interactions since that's tripping you up. Think about it this way: how might a policy that addresses flooding in Miami also affect housing — or vice versa? What's the connection between those two problems in Miami specifically?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: Good progress — the student has committed to two topics. Now they have a specific structural question about repetition across sections and the 'Interactions' field. Rather than explaining what Interactions means, I should ask a Socratic question that helps them discover it themselves. I'll focus just on Interactions since that's their stated sticking point, keeping it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I mean maybe flooding damages cheaper housing more, or pushes up costs, or if you upgrade infrastructure it changes neighborhoods? I’m not sure if that’s the kind of interaction they mean, or if they want like a whole explanation of how every climate policy connects to every other one.
exchanges.tutor: Those are exactly the kinds of connections worth exploring. You don't need to map every policy to every other one — just the meaningful overlaps relevant to your two components. You now have your two topics and some real ideas. Try drafting just the Context section for your flooding component — a few sentences about Miami's current situation. What would you write?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually stumbled onto exactly the right kind of thinking for Interactions — they just don't recognize it yet. I should affirm the direction of their thinking without confirming it as a complete answer, and push them to trust it and apply it. I don't need to explain more; I should redirect them toward writing.

exchanges.student: I’m still blanking a little. Could you just show me what one component sort of looks like in plain terms, like not a full polished answer, just the shape of it?
exchanges.tutor: That's not something I can do — even a rough model risks becoming a template you fill in rather than ideas you develop. But remember, the assignment itself includes examples for reference — have you looked at those? And you already have something: 'Miami faces flooding that disproportionately affects lower-income housing.' That's a Context sentence. What would come next?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to model a component for them, which would cross the line into doing the work for them. I should decline clearly but not harshly, and redirect them back to their own ideas. They already have real content — flooding, housing, Miami — and I should push them to use it. The assignment also mentions examples are provided to students, so I can point them there.

exchanges.student: I looked at them but then mine feels too simple compared to those. I think I’m overthinking it and also not sure how formal this is supposed to sound, so I kind of just want a general explanation of what makes a strong component versus a weak one.
exchanges.tutor: Feeling like yours is 'too simple' is very common, but I can't help you with tone or strength in the abstract — I can only respond to something you've actually written. You have a sentence already: 'Miami faces flooding that disproportionately affects lower-income housing.' Keep going from there. Write 3-4 more sentences for the Context section and share them here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stalling again, comparing themselves to examples and asking for a general rubric rather than writing. This is the same avoidance pattern. I should be gently firm, acknowledge the feeling but redirect to action. We're also getting close to the planned conversation length, so I should push toward actual writing. I won't grade or evaluate abstractly — I'll push them to produce something I can respond to concretely.

exchanges.student: I’m not really sure what 3–4 sentences should even cover though. Like is Context just background, or is it also supposed to hint at the solution and the EJ part too?
exchanges.tutor: The assignment tells you: Context is 'current situation and key challenge.' That's it — save EJ and solutions for their own sections. You already know Miami, flooding, and housing inequality. Stop planning and start writing — even something rough. What's one sentence about Miami's current flooding situation?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a reasonable clarifying question about the Context section's scope. However, this is also a pattern of seeking permission/clarity before writing rather than just attempting it. The assignment structure is clear: Context is 'current situation and key challenge' — that's all. I should point them back to the assignment description briefly and firmly push them to write rather than plan. We're near the end of the planned conversation length so I need to move toward a writing attempt.
